metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'enforcement' flag (module path, name, type, and how to toggle it in tests) in the Implementation section so ACs 17-18 are measurable.
evidence: 'Pin collection runs identically with enforcement enabled or disabled'
'Enabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership'
'Disabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership'

ABSENCE: no module, flag name, type, or toggle mechanism for 'enforcement' is specified anywhere in the plan; child 7c27 is mentioned as owning 'prerequisite criterion' but the enforcement flag's interface is never named
findings.finding: The terms 'enforcement enabled' and 'enforcement disabled' appear in ACs 17-18 and the implementation ('Pin collection runs identically with enforcement enabled or disabled') but the plan never defines what 'enforcement' is — what flag or setting controls it, which module owns it, or how a test toggles it. An executor cannot write the AC 17-18 tests without knowing the flag's identity, type, and location.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria lines 17-18 ('Enabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership', 'Disabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership') and Implementation bullet 6
scenarios: Executor writing test_plan_review_resign.py cannot parameterize 'enforcement enabled/disabled' without knowing the flag or its API surface
AC 17-18 cannot be evaluated in-session because the observable toggle is undefined
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'Enforcement refers to the boolean flag plan_review.config.PREREQUISITE_ENFORCEMENT_ENABLED (or equivalent); tests toggle it via monkeypatch or a constructor argument to the signing path under test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the activation contradiction: either (a) explicitly state that pin collection IS wired into signing paths in this story (making the 'optional pins preserve old behavior' clause refer only to the public deployment gate, not the code path), or (b) defer signing-path wiring to child 5967 and remove ACs 19–21 from this story.
evidence: 'Every plan-review signature-producing path…calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id, repo_root) immediately before building its new manifest'
'No new plan-review attestation can be minted pinless'
'optional pins preserve old behavior until child 5967 atomically activates relation collection across all signing paths'
AC: 'Ordinary signing emits the complete pin set'
findings.finding: The Implementation section simultaneously asserts two mutually contradictory activation semantics: (a) 'Every plan-review signature-producing path—ordinary sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, and resign_plan_review—calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot…immediately before building its new manifest' and 'No new plan-review attestation can be minted pinless', yet (b) 'optional pins preserve old behavior until child 5967 atomically activates relation collection across all signing paths.' Statement (a) implies pin collection is wired and mandatory in this story; statement (b) implies it is deferred and optional. The ACs then affirm (a): 'Ordinary signing emits the complete pin set' (AC 19). Implementers will receive conflicting directives.
findings.location: Implementation section (paragraph 5 and 6) vs AC lines 19-21
scenarios: An implementer wires collect_plan_relation_snapshot into signing paths (satisfying ACs 19–21) and finds the 'optional pins' clause makes the feature-branch safety claim false.
An implementer reads 'optional pins preserve old behavior' and does NOT wire collection, failing ACs 19–21 and the test in test_plan_review_resign.py.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'optional pins preserve old behavior until child 5967 atomically activates relation collection across all signing paths' with 'pin collection is wired into all signing paths in this story; the feature branch is not merged to main until child 5967 completes the atomic integration tests, but the code paths are live on the feature branch and covered by this story's tests.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the exact activation boundary: either (a) this story wires all collection calls and child 5967 only adds the retry/lock/atomic-commit layer on top of already-emitting paths, OR (b) this story only defines the types and helpers and child 5967 wires the actual calls — but not both simultaneously.
evidence: Plan: 'Every plan-review signature-producing path … calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id, repo_root) immediately before building its new manifest and passes related_material'
Plan: 'optional pins preserve old behavior until child 5967 atomically activates relation collection across all signing paths, and the feature is not deployed between those changes'
AC: 'Ordinary signing emits the complete pin set' — this AC only makes sense if pins are actually emitted by this story

Child 5967 ticket description owns 'Unified pre-sign transaction … Snapshot phase, own material, and PlanRelationSnapshot before the LLM' and the retry/lock machinery — suggesting 5967 is where collection is truly activated in production
findings.finding: The plan contains an unresolved ambiguity about which signing paths actually emit pins after this story lands versus after child 5967. It simultaneously states (a) 'Every plan-review signature-producing path—ordinary sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, and resign_plan_review—calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot … immediately before building its new manifest and passes related_material' (this story's Implementation) and (b) 'optional pins preserve old behavior until child 5967 atomically activates relation collection across all signing paths.' If this story wires the collection calls into all signing paths, then pins ARE being emitted after this story — making child 5967's 'atomic activation' role unclear. If child 5967 is the actual activation switch, then this story does NOT wire the calls into production signing paths — contradicting (a). The plan never clarifies the mechanism by which pin collection is 'optional' when the collection call is unconditional.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Every plan-review signature-producing path … calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot … immediately before building its new manifest' vs. 'this story lands only on the epic feature branch: optional pins preserve old behavior until child 5967 atomically activates relation collection across all signing paths'
scenarios: An implementer reads this story and wires collect_plan_relation_snapshot into sign_plan_review NOW (per (a)), but child 5967 then tries to 'atomically activate' the same call — resulting in duplicate collection or conflicting wiring

An implementer interprets (b) as 'don't wire the calls yet' and leaves the collection paths as stubs — making AC 'Ordinary signing emits the complete pin set' untestable in this story's verification suite

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'enforcement' mechanism — name the config key, feature flag, or code path that toggles it — and co-locate its vocabulary with the AC/Implementation section that references it.
evidence: Plan states: 'Pin collection runs identically with enforcement enabled or disabled'
AC 17: 'Enabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership'
AC 18: 'Disabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership'
No config key, feature flag, or protocol definition for 'enforcement' appears anywhere in the plan
Codebase search for 'enforce_plan_material_pins' returns no matches
findings.finding: The 'enforcement enabled/disabled' decision rule is referenced in ACs 17-18 and the Implementation ('Pin collection runs identically with enforcement enabled or disabled') but is never defined — no config key, flag name, code path, or enum vocabulary specifying what 'enforcement' means or how it is toggled.
findings.location: Implementation §5 ('Pin collection runs identically with enforcement enabled or disabled'); AC lines 17-18 ('Enabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership', 'Disabled enforcement does not change emitted pin membership')
scenarios: An executor implementing the signing paths cannot determine what 'enforcement enabled' means — what code path, config flag, or environment variable to toggle — making ACs 17-18 untestable and implementation ambiguous.
The test file test_plan_review_resign.py is expected to prove 'enforcement both enabled and disabled' but has no spec to target.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence naming the control: e.g. 'Enforcement is the `require_plan_material_pins` config boolean (default False on this branch; True after child 5967 activates it). With enforcement disabled, pin collection still runs and pins are still emitted, but a PlanRelationSnapshotError does not block signing — it is logged and the review proceeds pinless.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between 'No new plan-review attestation can be minted pinless' and 'optional pins preserve old behavior until child 5967': explicitly state whether this story (a) wires and enforces pin collection on all signing paths immediately on the feature branch, or (b) wires collection but keeps the enforcement gate off until child 5967 activates it, and update the relevant AC to match.
evidence: 'No new plan-review attestation can be minted pinless.'
'optional pins preserve old behavior until child 5967 atomically activates relation collection across all signing paths, and the feature is not deployed between those changes.'
These two statements directly contradict each other about the enforcement posture of this story.
findings.finding: The plan contains an internal contradiction on whether pins are enforced in this story: one sentence states 'No new plan-review attestation can be minted pinless,' but a later sentence states 'optional pins preserve old behavior until child 5967 atomically activates relation collection across all signing paths.' An implementer reading both will not know whether this story makes pins mandatory or optional, and the ACs ('Ordinary signing emits the complete pin set') do not resolve whether that emission is enforced at signing time or only present as optional infrastructure.
findings.location: Implementation section — paragraphs 5 and 6; AC lines 'Ordinary signing emits the complete pin set', 'No new plan-review attestation can be minted pinless'
scenarios: An implementer wires pin collection into all signing paths (per the first statement) and adds a guard that rejects pinless manifests, then realizes the second statement says legacy pinless behavior must still work, causing rework.

A reviewer testing AC 'Ordinary signing emits the complete pin set' passes the test, but the feature-branch integration fails because the 'optional pins preserve old behavior' path was not implemented.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single explicit sentence such as: 'On this feature branch, all three signing paths (sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, resign_plan_review) collect and emit pins unconditionally; the phrase "optional pins preserve old behavior" refers only to the main-branch state before child 5967 merges this branch — not to any code path in this story.' Then update the AC to read 'Ordinary signing on the feature branch emits the complete pin set' to make the scope unambiguous.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the imprecise 'follows snapshot_head rev-parse behavior' anchor with a standalone contract: tracker_head_sha runs `git -C <tracker> rev-parse HEAD`, never resolves a branch ref or returns EMPTY_REPO_SENTINEL, and raises PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason=store-read-failure) on every failure path (including empty-repo).
evidence: Existing snapshot_head in src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_concurrency.py:41-63: resolves via tickets_branch first, falls back to 'HEAD', returns EMPTY_REPO_SENTINEL on empty repo — no cleanliness checks, no exceptions raised

Plan text: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) follows snapshot_head rev-parse behavior but adds new cleanliness checks: after ensure_fresh, run git -C <tracker> status --porcelain and git -C <tracker> ls-files -u; any output raises PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason=store-read-failure)'
snapshot_head returns EMPTY_REPO_SENTINEL on failure; tracker_head_sha must raise — opposite behaviors
findings.finding: The plan says tracker_head_sha 'follows snapshot_head rev-parse behavior' — but the existing snapshot_head function (rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_concurrency.py) resolves a tickets-branch ref first (via tickets_branch()), falls back to HEAD, and returns EMPTY_REPO_SENTINEL (not an exception) when neither resolves. tracker_head_sha is specified to run 'git -C <tracker> rev-parse HEAD' directly and raise PlanRelationSnapshotError on any failure. These are materially different contracts: snapshot_head does no cleanliness checks, never raises, and can return a sentinel. An implementer who takes 'follows snapshot_head behavior' literally could omit the required cleanliness checks or adopt the EMPTY_REPO_SENTINEL fallback instead of raising store-read-failure.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 5: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) follows snapshot_head rev-parse behavior but adds new cleanliness checks'
scenarios: An implementer models tracker_head_sha on the actual snapshot_head code and omits dirty/unmerged checks or swallows failures into a sentinel return, violating AC: 'Dirty, unmerged, subprocess, IO, path, or malformed-output failures map to store-read-failure'
Tests written against EMPTY_REPO_SENTINEL semantics instead of store-read-failure semantics
findings.suggested_fix: Drop 'follows snapshot_head rev-parse behavior' and state the full self-contained contract: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) runs git -C <tracker> status --porcelain and git -C <tracker> ls-files -u after ensure_fresh; any output raises PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason=store-read-failure, reference=<tracker>). Then runs git -C <tracker> rev-parse HEAD; the output must be exactly one 40-lowercase-hex SHA — any other output, nonzero exit, missing git, invalid path, or IO error also raises the same error. EMPTY_REPO_SENTINEL is never returned.'

findings.finding: refresh_attestation (attest.py lines 350-385) is one of the three named signing paths that must emit a complete pin set, but unlike sign_plan_review or resign_plan_review it reconstructs a manifest from the PRIOR manifest (not a fresh PlanContext/verdict) and accepts no ticket-state input. The plan says 'Every plan-review signature-producing path … collects the same PlanRelationSnapshot and passes related_material', yet it never specifies how refresh_attestation — whose signature is (ticket_id, prior_manifest, *, probe, repo_root) with no depends_on/blocks scan context — acquires the PlanRelationSnapshot.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Extend build_manifest(…, *, pins …)' / attest.py refresh_attestation (lines 350-385)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly assign `collect_plan_relation_snapshot` to its module (e.g., plan_review/relation_snapshot.py) in the Implementation section.
evidence: 'Every plan-review signature-producing path … calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id, repo_root) immediately before building its new manifest'

ABSENCE: no module assignment for collect_plan_relation_snapshot in any bullet; plan_review/relation_snapshot.py is listed as defining PlanRelationSnapshot, PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshotError, and tracker_head_sha() only
findings.finding: The function `collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id, repo_root)` is called in Implementation bullet 6 across all three signing paths, but no module is specified as its home. The plan defines PlanRelationSnapshot and related symbols in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py, but never explicitly states that collect_plan_relation_snapshot also lives there.
findings.location: Implementation bullet 6: 'calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id, repo_root) immediately before building its new manifest'
scenarios: Executor may place the function in attest.py, orchestration, or relation_snapshot.py based on guess, leading to inconsistent import structure or a failing import in the signing paths
findings.suggested_fix: In Implementation bullet 2, add: 'Also define collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id, repo_root) -> PlanRelationSnapshot in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py; it is the sole public entry point for building the snapshot.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback path or expand-contract description for the refresh/resign behavior change: either document a manual repair procedure, a grace-mode flag that restores prior behavior, or a back-out step that allows the feature branch to be reverted without data loss.
evidence: 'rather than preserving stale certification' confirms this is an intentional behavioral break from the prior contract
No rollback mechanism, feature-flag guard for this specific behavior change, or back-out procedure is described
ABSENCE: no AC or Verification line addresses how to recover a review that is permanently blocked by a now-deleted child
findings.finding: The plan changes the behavior of refresh_attestation and resign_plan_review: where both previously succeeded for reviews with deleted targets, they now return unsigned INDETERMINATE with reason missing-target. This is a breaking change to an existing interface. The plan acknowledges the new behavior but provides no rollback path, back-out plan, or expand-contract sequencing — only the operational note 'until the stale relationship is repaired.' Acknowledgement alone is insufficient under T4.
findings.location: Implementation section paragraph 5: 'If a referenced target was deleted after the prior PASS, refresh and re-sign return the same unsigned INDETERMINATE missing-target remediation until the stale relationship is repaired, rather than preserving stale certification.'
scenarios: A user with a certified review whose child ticket was archived/deleted can no longer refresh or resign that review without first repairing the stale relationship; with no rollback path, previously-usable reviews become permanently stuck until a manual graph repair is performed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section documenting the PlanRelationSnapshot isolation rule, the plan-material-pin manifest format, the PlanRelationSnapshotError reason enum, and the rationale for forbidding partial pin sets before child 5967 activates the feature.
evidence: ABSENCE: no mention of ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design doc anywhere in the plan
"Define PlanRelationSnapshot … in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py, which imports no signing or orchestration modules" — architectural rule with no recorded rationale
"Child 5967 exclusively owns … atomic append+commit" — cross-story dependency with no design record
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural pattern — a new typed boundary (PlanRelationSnapshot) between relation discovery and plan signing, a new manifest line format (plan-material-pin), and a new error taxonomy (PlanRelationSnapshotError with six named reasons) — but does not mention capturing this decision in an ADR, AGENTS.md, or design doc. With child 5967 activating this across all signing paths, future maintainers have no documented rationale for why relation discovery is isolated, why archived sources must be scanned, or why partial pin sets are forbidden.
findings.location: Implementation section (relation_snapshot.py module definition and signing-path integration)
scenarios: A new contributor modifying the signing pipeline does not know why collect_plan_relation_snapshot must run before manifest build, why the one-way import rule exists, or what breaks if partial pins are allowed — leading to inadvertent coupling violations.
When child 5967 activates this feature, reviewers of that PR have no design doc to validate the activation contract against.
findings.suggested_fix: Create docs/adr/NNN-plan-material-pins.md covering: (1) why relation discovery is in a module that imports no signing code, (2) why the O(store) preload is required rather than a targeted lookup, (3) the pin ordering and deduplication contract, and (4) the handoff contract with child 5967.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a happy-path structured log event (e.g., `{event: plan_pins_collected, subject_canonical_id, child_count, prerequisite_count}`) emitted after successful collect_plan_relation_snapshot so that zero-pin outcomes for non-leaf tickets are observable.
evidence: "emits an ERROR record exactly {event: plan_relation_snapshot_error, reason: <enum>, canonical_id: string|null, reference: string|null}" — only error-path record specified
ABSENCE: no happy-path structured log event (e.g., {event: plan_pins_collected, subject: ..., child_count: N, prerequisite_count: M}) is mentioned anywhere in the plan
"No new plan-review attestation can be minted pinless" — the invariant is stated but no runtime observability confirms it was enforced
findings.finding: The plan specifies structured ERROR records only for the failure path (PlanRelationSnapshotError). On the happy path, there is no specified log/metric/trace emitted when pin collection succeeds and pins are written to the manifest. If a bug causes relation discovery to silently return an empty set for a ticket that legitimately has children or prerequisites, operators and tests have no signal to distinguish 'correctly zero relations' from 'silent collection failure'.
findings.location: Implementation section — 'Run pin collection before prerequisite LLM evaluation' and Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A regression in alias resolution causes child IDs to fail canonicalization silently (no error, just missing entries); the manifest is signed with zero pins and passes all format checks, with no log entry to surface the anomaly.
During oncall investigation of a mis-signed manifest, there is no structured event to grep for to confirm what pins were collected at signing time.
findings.suggested_fix: After collect_plan_relation_snapshot returns successfully, emit a structured INFO record {event: plan_pins_collected, subject_canonical_id: <id>, child_count: <N>, prerequisite_count: <M>} so that unexpected empty sets are detectable in logs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm tracker_head_sha() delegates its git status, git ls-files -u, and git rev-parse calls through the existing run_git() helper (src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py) rather than opening new subprocess calls inline — llm/code_review/assemble.py already demonstrates that run_git is importable at this layer.
evidence: src/rebar/_store/gitutil.py line 7: 'each call site keeps its OWN return/error contract by adapting the returned CompletedProcess locally' — run_git was created to replace 'a dozen hand-rolled wrappers'

src/rebar/llm/code_review/assemble.py line 25: 'from rebar._store.gitutil import run_git' — the llm/ layer already imports run_git, so the import restriction ('imports no signing or orchestration modules') does not block using run_git here

The plan writes 'git -C <tracker> status --porcelain', 'git -C <tracker> ls-files -u', 'git -C <tracker> rev-parse HEAD' as three separate described commands — indicating new inline subprocess calls rather than run_git delegation
findings.finding: tracker_head_sha() in relation_snapshot.py reimplements git subprocess invocation that run_git() already provides, despite run_git being importable at the llm/ layer (rebar.llm.code_review.assemble already imports it from rebar._store.gitutil). run_git was introduced precisely to eliminate 'a dozen hand-rolled wrappers' with divergent signatures; adding a new inline subprocess block for git status, git ls-files, and git rev-parse repeats the original fragmentation.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) follows snapshot_head rev-parse behavior but adds new cleanliness checks: after ensure_fresh, run git -C <tracker> status --porcelain and git -C <tracker> ls-files -u'
scenarios: tracker_head_sha() diverges from run_git's error-handling contract (timeout, text=True vs bytes, index.lock retry) requiring re-learning and potential inconsistency
A future change to run_git (e.g. adding a timeout or tracing) will not propagate to tracker_head_sha()
findings.suggested_fix: In plan_review/relation_snapshot.py, import run_git from rebar._store.gitutil and use it for all three git invocations, adapting returncode/stdout locally, consistent with every other _store-layer git consumer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm PlanRelationSnapshot warrants a named typed dataclass now rather than a plain return value (dict or NamedTuple) that child 7c27 can later formalize into a typed boundary when the second consumer lands — or explicitly document why the typed seam is load-bearing for the child 7c27 interface contract at this story's boundary.
evidence: The plan names only two consumers of PlanRelationSnapshot: (1) pin emission in sign paths (this story), (2) child 7c27 prerequisite criterion (explicitly deferred and not implemented here)

Plan quote: 'Child 7c27 owns registration, schemas, prompts, and routing for the future prerequisite criterion; this story exposes PlanRelationSnapshot as its typed input boundary but does not implement or assume that criterion exists'
No existing call-sites found in codebase (search for 'PlanRelationSnapshot' returns zero src/ hits)
findings.finding: PlanRelationSnapshot is a named typed dataclass with exactly one concrete call-site at this story's landing boundary — the sign-path pin-collection caller. The only other declared consumer (child 7c27's prerequisite criterion) is explicitly deferred ('this story exposes PlanRelationSnapshot as its typed input boundary but does not implement or assume that criterion exists'). This does not satisfy the Rule-of-Three for a non-trivial abstraction (named dataclass with 6 fields and a dedicated module).
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Define PlanRelationSnapshot, PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshotError, and tracker_head_sha() in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py'
scenarios: If child 7c27 is cancelled or restructured, PlanRelationSnapshot remains a module with a single consumer — the whole typed boundary was introduced speculatively
Child 7c27 may evolve the schema and force a redesign of the boundary anyway, so the early abstraction saves no work

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name `resign.py` as an edit target in the Implementation section and specify where in `resign_plan_review`'s call sequence `collect_plan_relation_snapshot` is called and where `PlanRelationSnapshotError` is caught and converted to the structured unsigned INDETERMINATE return.
evidence: resign.py line 126: `sig = attest.sign_plan_review(verdict, material=current_material, repo_root=repo_root)` — the only signing call; if collection happens inside sign_plan_review, the error would surface as an exception from sign_plan_review rather than the structured INDETERMINATE return the plan requires.

Plan: 'the review boundary catches it before any LLM call, emits an ERROR record exactly {event: plan_relation_snapshot_error, …}, and returns unsigned INDETERMINATE remediation through CLI and MCP' — resign.py has its own return-dict format and no LLM calls, so it is the boundary.
Plan Implementation section names only relation_snapshot.py, manifest.py, attest.py, and build_manifest as explicit edit targets; resign.py is absent.
findings.finding: resign.py is an implicit edit target — the plan requires resign_plan_review to call collect_plan_relation_snapshot and to return unsigned INDETERMINATE on PlanRelationSnapshotError before any LLM call — but it is not named in the Implementation section as a file to modify. The current resign_plan_review delegates to attest.sign_plan_review at line 126; the error boundary ('the review boundary catches it before any LLM call') must sit at the resign_plan_review call site, not buried inside sign_plan_review, because resign.py has its own NO-LLM contract and its own return-dict shape.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Every plan-review signature-producing path … calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot'; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/resign.py
scenarios: Implementer places PlanRelationSnapshotError catch only inside sign_plan_review, causing resign_plan_review to raise rather than return unsigned INDETERMINATE — violating the structured-error AC.
Implementer leaves resign.py unmodified and the pin collection call is entirely absent from the resign path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the Verification section, mark the three non-existent test modules as new files to be created as part of this story, so it is clear they are deliverables not pre-existing.
evidence: Search for test_plan_material_pins, test_plan_relation_snapshot, test_plan_review_resign in repo — zero hits in tests/unit/.
Only test_attest_opcert.py is confirmed to exist (tests/unit/test_attest_opcert.py, confirmed by search).

The Verification section: 'pytest -q tests/unit/test_plan_material_pins.py tests/unit/test_plan_relation_snapshot.py tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py' — presented as a verification command without noting these files must be created.
findings.finding: Three of the four test modules cited in the Verification section (tests/unit/test_plan_material_pins.py, tests/unit/test_plan_relation_snapshot.py, tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py) do not exist in the repository. The Verification section presents them as runnable pytest commands without flagging them as new files to be created, which could lead an implementer to run those commands expecting existing test infrastructure and instead see collection errors.
findings.location: Verification § pytest -q commands
scenarios: A CI check or reviewer runs the Verification commands before the new test files are committed and gets a 'no such file' error rather than a meaningful test failure, making verification status ambiguous.
findings.suggested_fix: Prefix each new-file test module with a note such as '(new file, created by this story)' or move them to the Implementation section's list of deliverables.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for why refresh_attestation and resign_plan_review cannot accept a caller-supplied PlanRelationSnapshot (or reuse a targeted K-ID fetch), explicitly acknowledging and accepting the O(store) latency cost these previously-cheap paths now bear.
evidence: Plan text: 'refresh_attestation gains no caller-supplied relation state'
resign.py docstring: 'NO LLM and NO network — a sidecar read, a light fingerprint recompute, and a local HMAC sign'

The child/prerequisite IDs are already present in the subject's compiled state (deps list + parent_id tree), so a targeted fetch of only those IDs would require O(K) reduces (K=direct relations) not an O(store) full scan — but the plan rejects this without explanation
reduce_all_tickets in reducer/_api.py iterates every directory in the tracker, which for large stores is significantly more expensive than the current refresh/resign operations
findings.finding: The plan mandates that refresh_attestation 'gains no caller-supplied relation state' and that resign_plan_review calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot immediately before building its manifest, but provides no positive rationale for rejecting caller-supplied snapshots. This forces both the drift-refresh path (previously a cheap dep-rehash: no LLM, no network, ~1 dep-map read) and resign_plan_review (explicitly designed as 'NO LLM and NO network — a sidecar read, a light fingerprint recompute, and a local HMAC sign') to unconditionally bear a full O(store) preload cost. The plan gives no rationale for why snapshot reuse — or lazy collection limited to the child/prerequisite IDs already in the subject's compiled state — was rejected, leaving the latency/cost impact unjustified.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Every plan-review signature-producing path … refresh_attestation gains no caller-supplied relation state'; AC: 'Progressive drift refresh emits the complete current pin set' / 'Re-signing emits the complete current pin set'
scenarios: A store with thousands of tickets: drift-refresh (previously <100ms) now triggers a full O(N) reduce_all_tickets preload on every code-drift refresh, potentially adding seconds of latency
resign_plan_review called as a cheap recovery operation now silently acquires the same full store scan cost, surprising operators who depend on its documented 'fast, no-LLM' contract

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the O(store) full-preload design vs. the established byte-scan+selective-reduce pattern in `reducer/_inbound.py`, either with a measured cost comparison, a documented reason the byte-scan approach cannot be adapted to include archived sources, or an explicit acceptance of the tradeoff for implementation simplicity.
evidence: Plan: 'do not call find_inbound_relationships because its active-source filter intentionally omits archived tickets'

src/rebar/reducer/_inbound.py:103: `if state.get('status') in _INACTIVE_SOURCE_STATUSES or state.get('archived'): continue` — confirms _inbound.py filters archived *sources*, making it unsuitable as-is
src/rebar/reducer/_inbound.py:39-72: _mentioning_dirs uses byte-scan rather than full reduction — the established two-phase pattern avoids full-store O(N) reduces
src/rebar/reducer/_api.py:215-280: reduce_all_tickets exists and would implement the full preload, but it reduces every ticket unconditionally
ABSENCE: no analysis in the plan of store size sensitivity or comparison to a byte-scan variant that preserves archived sources
findings.finding: The plan mandates an O(store) full-preload of every ticket directory (including archived) to build ticket_states_by_id, explicitly rejecting the established two-phase approach in reducer/_inbound.py (byte-scan candidates + selective reduce). The plan states this is 'required to discover arbitrary inbound blockers,' but the existing _inbound.py approach only filters on archived *source* status — a modified byte-scan restricted to `blocks` relation events could still find archived sources while avoiding a full-store reduce. The novelty of the O(store) preload vs. the established two-phase scan pattern is not justified with a cost comparison or acknowledgment that the existing pattern could be adapted.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Build exactly one store-wide ticket_states_by_id preload per review by reducing every ticket directory, including archived states; this explicit O(store) map is required to discover arbitrary inbound blockers.'
scenarios: Large deployments with thousands of tickets incur full-store reduction on every plan-review signing, even for tickets with zero inbound blockers — whereas the existing two-phase approach only reduces tickets that byte-mention the subject ID.

If the byte-scan+selective-reduce approach were extended to not filter archived sources (remove the `state.get('archived')` branch), it would satisfy the archived-inbound-blocker requirement at lower cost. The plan does not consider or rule out this adaptation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a backward-compatibility assertion: specify that pre-feature parsers tolerate (ignore or accept) unknown 'plan-material-pin:' lines in stored manifests, or document the explicit rollback remediation step (e.g., re-sign affected attestations with the old code path after rollback), and assert the exact schema state of persisted attestations after a code rollback.
evidence: Plan specifies pre-feature-verifier fixture for NEW code reading OLD manifests, but is silent on OLD code reading NEW manifests after rollback.

Plan says 'optional pins preserve old behavior until child 5967 atomically activates' — but does not describe what happens to already-minted pin-containing attestations if the feature branch is reverted.
T11(e) requires: 'the plan must assert on the schema STATE AFTER rollback, not merely that rollback exits 0'
ABSENCE: no backward-compat fixture or parse-tolerance guarantee for pre-feature code encountering post-feature manifests.
findings.finding: The plan specifies forward compatibility (new-reader parses legacy pinless manifests) but never addresses backward compatibility: if code is rolled back after pin-containing attestations have been committed to the tracker, old code's manifest parser will encounter 'plan-material-pin:' lines it was not built to handle. The plan does not assert what the persisted format state looks like after such a rollback, leaving those attestations potentially unverifiable or rejected by the prior parser.
findings.location: Implementation § 'Preserve new-reader parsing of legacy manifests with no pins' and AC 'A pre-feature reader authenticates the complete stored manifest, then ignores additive pin lines for field interpretation'
scenarios: Feature branch is merged and a signing operation mints several attestations with pin lines. A production incident triggers a code rollback to the pre-feature version. Old code's manifest parser, which has no handling for 'plan-material-pin:' lines, now encounters new attestations and either rejects them or silently misparses them, rendering those attestations unverifiable or inconsistently authenticated.
A CI pipeline still running on the old code version attempts to verify a freshly-signed attestation containing pin lines; verification fails with an unknown-field error, blocking dependent workflows.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the pre-feature-verifier fixture (or add a companion fixture) to run the *old* manifest-parsing code against a *new* manifest containing pin lines, asserting it does not raise and still authenticates correctly. If the old parser is strict, document a compensating-forward rollback procedure: after reverting code, re-sign or strip pin lines from affected attestations, and note this explicitly in the rollback runbook.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify git status --porcelain behavior specifically against the tickets-tracker repo layout (orphan branch / bare style) and confirm whether --porcelain=v1 with explicit pathspec or `--untracked-files=no` scoping is needed to avoid false store-read-failure on clean trackers.
evidence: Partially-verified: git status --porcelain is a well-known stable git subcommand, but it is NOT present anywhere in the src/rebar codebase (confirmed by search), making this a new integration surface for this project.
git ls-files -u IS already used at src/rebar/_store/push.py:92 (Verified).

The tickets tracker is a git worktree or orphan-branch repo; git status --porcelain behavior on a tracker with no working tree (bare or worktree-only) may differ from a normal checkout — for example, it may not report unstaged changes to tracked files if there is no index.

The plan also says 'any output raises PlanRelationSnapshotError' — but git status --porcelain may emit output for untracked files or ignored files depending on flags, which could produce false store-read-failure errors in some tracker configurations.
findings.finding: git status --porcelain is named as a new cleanliness check in tracker_head_sha() but is not currently invoked anywhere in the codebase; its exact output behavior (e.g., whether it covers untracked files in a bare-style worktree-only tracker repo) is unverified for this specific tracker layout.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) follows snapshot_head rev-parse behavior but adds new cleanliness checks: after ensure_fresh, run git -C <tracker> status --porcelain and git -C <tracker> ls-files -u'
scenarios: A tickets tracker configured as a bare or orphan-branch repository emits unexpected git status --porcelain output (e.g., for untracked files), causing spurious store-read-failure rejections on every signing attempt.
A CI environment where the tracker worktree has no index causes git status --porcelain to behave differently than a normal checkout, producing false positives.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `git -C <tracker> status --porcelain=v1 --untracked-files=no` to suppress untracked-file noise from the porcelain output, consistent with the intent of detecting only dirty index or working-tree modifications to tracked files.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define PlanRelationSnapshotError.reason as a Literal type or Enum in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py (e.g. `Reason = Literal['missing-target', 'ambiguous-reference', 'malformed-reference', 'reducer-error', 'canonical-id-mismatch', 'store-read-failure']`) and reference that type in the exception class spec.
evidence: Plan lists six reason strings inline: 'missing-target, ambiguous-reference, malformed-reference, reducer-error, canonical-id-mismatch, or store-read-failure'
No Literal[], Enum, or TypedDict for reason is specified — the plan only defines PlanRelationSnapshotError as a class with 'reason' and optional 'target reference' attributes
The structured ERROR record references '<enum>' as the reason value, confirming the intent that reason is enumerated, but the enum type is never defined
Codebase search confirms plan_review/relation_snapshot.py does not exist yet
findings.finding: PlanRelationSnapshotError.reason is treated throughout the plan as a closed enum (six exact string values enumerated inline), but no formal type definition — Literal type, Enum class, or schema constant — is specified or co-located with the exception class definition in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py. The AC ('Every named PlanRelationSnapshotError reason produces the exact structured ERROR record') and Verification ('proves…every exact abort reason') both key on exact string matching without a canonical source of truth.
findings.location: Implementation §4 ('reason is exactly missing-target, ambiguous-reference, malformed-reference, reducer-error, canonical-id-mismatch, or store-read-failure'); AC line 12 ('Every named PlanRelationSnapshotError reason produces the exact structured ERROR record without a traceback fallback')
scenarios: An executor implementing PlanRelationSnapshotError and the review-boundary catch must hardcode six string literals without a canonical type reference, risking typos that break AC 12.
Test authors writing test_plan_relation_snapshot.py must independently re-enumerate the six strings without a shared constant, creating divergence risk.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the PlanRelationSnapshotError definition: 'reason: Literal["missing-target", "ambiguous-reference", "malformed-reference", "reducer-error", "canonical-id-mismatch", "store-read-failure"]; reference: str | None = None'. Export the Reason Literal as a module-level alias so tests can import it.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that `tracker_head_sha(tracker)` 'reuses the repository git helper to run git rev-parse HEAD against the tickets tracker … and rejects dirty or unmerged state.' The only existing git-HEAD helper, `signing.head_sha(repo_root)`, runs `git -C <repo_root> rev-parse HEAD` but performs NO dirty or unmerged-state check anywhere. No other helper in the codebase combines both behaviors. The dirty/unmerged rejection is entirely new implementation work, not a reuse of anything existing — the plan's 'reuses' framing is an unbacked codebase assertion.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) reuses the repository git helper to run git rev-parse HEAD against the tickets tracker after ensure_fresh and rejects dirty or unmerged state.'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'reuses the repository git helper' with 'calls `git -C <tracker> rev-parse HEAD` (following the pattern of `signing.head_sha`) and additionally runs `git -C <tracker> status --porcelain` and `git -C <tracker> ls-files -u`, raising a `PlanRelationSnapshotError` with reason `store-read-failure` on any dirty or unmerged output.'

findings.finding: The plan says PlanRelationSnapshot is 'built from one compiled ticket-state preload per review' and inbound blocks are found by 'directly scanning relation entries in every preloaded state' — but does not specify that this preload must cover ALL tickets in the store (not just the subject and named targets). The existing find_inbound_relationships does a byte-level O(store) scan precisely to discover arbitrary tickets that carry a 'blocks' dep pointing at the subject. If the preload covers only the subject + explicit targets, inbound blocks from arbitrary other tickets are silently missed, producing an incomplete prerequisite set.
findings.location: Implementation section: 'Build the snapshot from one compiled ticket-state preload per review. Collect outgoing depends_on from the subject and inbound blocks by directly scanning relation entries in every preloaded state'

findings.finding: tracker_head_sha() is specified to 'reuse the repository git helper' and 'rejects dirty or unmerged state,' but the plan does not specify how unexpected git infrastructure failures (e.g., git not installed, tracker path invalid, subprocess error) are handled — these could bypass PlanRelationSnapshotError and produce unhandled exceptions, contradicting the 'error never produces a traceback fallback' guarantee.
findings.location: Implementation section, paragraph 5: 'tracker_head_sha(tracker) reuses the repository git helper…rejects dirty or unmerged state.' / Paragraph 4: 'the error never produces a partial pin set or traceback fallback.'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Implementation section, add: 'tracker_head_sha wraps all subprocess and IO errors in PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason="store-read-failure", reference=tracker_path) so the review boundary's existing catch block handles them uniformly.' Add a corresponding AC: '[ ] tracker_head_sha subprocess failures raise PlanRelationSnapshotError(store-read-failure) rather than propagating raw exceptions.' Add a test case in test_plan_relation_snapshot.py for this scenario.

findings.finding: tracker_head_sha() is specified to reject dirty or unmerged tracker state, but the plan never names what exception it raises in those cases, never maps those states to a PlanRelationSnapshotError reason, and the test plan only asserts 'tracker HEAD capture' (the happy path). If dirty/unmerged raises something other than PlanRelationSnapshotError, the review boundary's catch — which the plan says must never produce a traceback fallback — will silently miss it.
findings.location: Implementation §5 ('tracker_head_sha... rejects dirty or unmerged state') and Verification §2 ('tracker HEAD capture')
findings.suggested_fix: Map dirty/unmerged state to store-read-failure (or add a new reason such as dirty-tracker-state) in PlanRelationSnapshotError, document this in the Implementation section, and add a test variant to test_plan_relation_snapshot.py covering 'tracker is dirty → store-read-failure ERROR record, no traceback'.

findings.finding: tracker_head_sha()'s 'dirty or unmerged state' rejection protocol is named but its exception type is never specified, leaving the review-boundary catch (which is keyed on PlanRelationSnapshotError) unable to guarantee pre-LLM termination and no-traceback-fallback for this failure mode.
findings.location: Implementation § tracker_head_sha / § review boundary catch / AC: 'Every unreadable or unresolvable target returns unsigned INDETERMINATE before any LLM call'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence: 'A dirty or unmerged tracker raises PlanRelationSnapshotError(reason="store-read-failure") with no target reference, so the review boundary's existing PlanRelationSnapshotError catch covers it.' This closes the enum/catch gap without adding a new reason value.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name the module that defines collect_plan_relation_snapshot and list it alongside the other exports in that module's spec; if it lives in relation_snapshot.py, confirm it does not import signing or orchestration modules.
evidence: 'calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id, repo_root) immediately before building its new manifest'
relation_snapshot.py is described as defining 'PlanRelationSnapshot, PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshotError, and tracker_head_sha()' — collect_plan_relation_snapshot absent
ABSENCE: no AC or Verification line names collect_plan_relation_snapshot as a tested symbol
findings.finding: The function collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id, repo_root) is called by all three signing paths in the Implementation but is never defined or assigned to any module in the plan. The spec for relation_snapshot.py enumerates only PlanRelationSnapshot, PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshotError, and tracker_head_sha(). No module is named as the home of collect_plan_relation_snapshot, leaving this deliverable without a covering definition or test anchor.
findings.location: Implementation section paragraph 5 ('calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot'); plan_review/relation_snapshot.py module spec paragraph 2
scenarios: An implementer does not know whether collect_plan_relation_snapshot belongs in relation_snapshot.py, a new orchestration module, or attest.py, leading to an ad-hoc placement that may violate the 'imports no signing or orchestration modules' constraint on relation_snapshot.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `collect_plan_relation_snapshot` to the `relation_snapshot.py` symbol inventory in the Implementation section, or explicitly state which module defines and exports it.
evidence: Plan text: 'Define PlanRelationSnapshot, PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshotError, and tracker_head_sha() in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py' — collect_plan_relation_snapshot is absent from this list.

Plan text: 'Every plan-review signature-producing path—ordinary sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, and resign_plan_review—calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id, repo_root) immediately before building its new manifest' — function is used but not homed.
No occurrence of collect_plan_relation_snapshot in the repo (search confirmed).
findings.finding: collect_plan_relation_snapshot is named throughout the plan as the function called from all three signing paths (sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, resign_plan_review) and in the Verification section, but it is never assigned to a module in the Implementation section. The plan's relation_snapshot.py symbol inventory lists PlanRelationSnapshot, PlanMaterialPin, PlanRelationSnapshotError, and tracker_head_sha — but not collect_plan_relation_snapshot. This leaves the implementer without a canonical module home for a central function.
findings.location: Implementation § relation_snapshot.py symbol inventory; Implementation § 'Every plan-review signature-producing path … calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot'
scenarios: Implementer creates collect_plan_relation_snapshot in the wrong module (e.g., attest.py), breaking the stated constraint that relation_snapshot.py 'imports no signing or orchestration modules'.
Implementer places it in relation_snapshot.py but tests import it from attest.py (which the plan implies via re-exports), causing import failures.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the relation_snapshot.py bullet: '…and collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id, repo_root) → PlanRelationSnapshot | raises PlanRelationSnapshotError. attest.py imports and re-exports it alongside the existing manifest helpers.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify collect_plan_relation_snapshot: its module (e.g. plan_review/relation_snapshot.py), full signature (parameters, return PlanRelationSnapshot), and whether it raises PlanRelationSnapshotError directly or wraps collection sub-errors.
evidence: Plan: 'every plan-review signature-producing path…calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id, repo_root) immediately before building its new manifest'
Codebase search for 'collect_plan_relation_snapshot' returns only .rebar/cache findings (prior review comments), no implementation
The plan defines PlanRelationSnapshot and tracker_head_sha in plan_review/relation_snapshot.py but never places collect_plan_relation_snapshot in any module
Return type is implicitly PlanRelationSnapshot but never stated; callers extract related_material from it but the extraction protocol is not specified
findings.finding: collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id, repo_root) is named as the call-site function invoked by all three signing paths (sign_plan_review, refresh_attestation, resign_plan_review) and referenced in the Verification test module, but is never defined: its module location, full signature, and return type are unspecified. The function does not exist in the codebase.
findings.location: Implementation §5 ('calls collect_plan_relation_snapshot(ticket_id, repo_root) immediately before building its new manifest'); Verification ('pytest -q tests/unit/test_plan_relation_snapshot.py')
scenarios: An executor implementing the three signing paths has no canonical module to import collect_plan_relation_snapshot from, no stated return type, and no specified error propagation contract (does it raise PlanRelationSnapshotError directly, or wrap it?).
The test file test_plan_relation_snapshot.py is described as proving 'canonical collection' but the function under test has no spec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add explicit failure-path test cases to test_plan_review_resign.py (or name a dedicated test) that verify refresh_attestation and resign_plan_review return unsigned INDETERMINATE with a structured plan_relation_snapshot_error event when a referenced target has been deleted after the prior PASS.
evidence: "pytest -q tests/unit/test_plan_review_resign.py proves ordinary sign, progressive drift refresh, and re-sign emit complete pins with enforcement both enabled and disabled" — no failure-path case named

"If a referenced target was deleted after the prior PASS, refresh and re-sign return the same unsigned INDETERMINATE missing-target remediation until the stale relationship is repaired" — failure path specified in Implementation but absent from Verification
AC 'Progressive drift refresh emits the complete current pin set' and 'Re-signing emits the complete current pin set' address only success paths
findings.finding: The Verification section names test_plan_review_resign.py as proving only the happy path ('emit complete pins with enforcement both enabled and disabled') for refresh_attestation and resign_plan_review, but the Implementation section introduces a specific failure path: 'if a referenced target was deleted after the prior PASS, refresh and re-sign return the same unsigned INDETERMINATE missing-target remediation.' This failure path on user-facing signing operations (refresh, re-sign) has no explicitly named test coverage in any verification target.
findings.location: Verification section / test_plan_review_resign.py description; Implementation section 'Extend build_manifest' paragraph
scenarios: A reviewer refreshes an attestation after a child ticket has been deleted; the signing path should return unsigned INDETERMINATE but this is not covered by any named test
Re-signing when a prerequisite ticket no longer exists should similarly abort — untested at the signing-path integration level
findings.suggested_fix: In the Verification bullet for test_plan_review_resign.py, append: '…and that refresh and re-sign return unsigned INDETERMINATE (missing-target) when a previously-valid target has been deleted.' Add corresponding ACs: 'Progressive drift refresh returns unsigned INDETERMINATE missing-target when a child or prerequisite has been deleted.' and 'Re-signing returns unsigned INDETERMINATE missing-target when a child or prerequisite has been deleted.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
